items.q: Is recycling the most environmentally beneficial waste strategy?
items.correct: No - the waste hierarchy puts reduction first: not using the material or energy at all avoids more impact than recycling, which itself consumes energy.
items.misconception: Yes - recycling is always the best option for the environment.
items.random_wrong: No - landfill is the most beneficial option.
items.why: Recycling has high visibility, so students rank it top. Collection, sorting and reprocessing all consume energy; reducing and reusing avoid those costs entirely.

items.q: Does renewable mean a resource can never run out?
items.correct: No - renewable resources can still be depleted if harvested faster than they regenerate, as overfishing and deforestation show.
items.misconception: Yes - renewable resources are unlimited by definition.
items.random_wrong: Yes - renewable just means the resource is free of charge.
items.why: Renewability is conditional on management. The regeneration rate sets a sustainable harvest limit; exceeding it degrades or destroys the resource.

items.q: If a material is biodegradable, is it automatically environmentally harmless?
items.correct: No - biodegradable materials may need specific conditions to break down, and in oxygen-poor landfill they can persist or release methane, a potent greenhouse gas.
items.misconception: Yes - biodegradable products simply vanish without any impact.
items.random_wrong: Yes - biodegradable materials cannot be made from oil.
items.why: The label suggests guilt-free disposal. Real decomposition depends on temperature, moisture and oxygen, and production impacts still exist regardless of disposal route.

items.q: A renewable resource is one that:
items.correct: Is replenished naturally at a rate comparable to its use, like responsibly managed timber
items.misconception: Can never be used up no matter how it is managed
items.random_wrong: Is always cheaper than non-renewable alternatives
items.why: Correct answer: Is replenished naturally at a rate comparable to its use, like responsibly managed timber. “Can never be used up no matter how it is managed” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: A product's environmental impact across its whole life, from raw materials to disposal
items.misconception: Only the recyclability of the packaging
items.random_wrong: Only the energy used in the factory
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A product's environmental impact across its whole life, from raw materials to disposal. “Only the recyclability of the packaging” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The total energy used to extract, process, manufacture and transport a material or product
items.misconception: The electricity a product uses when switched on
items.random_wrong: The calories needed by the factory workers
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The total energy used to extract, process, manufacture and transport a material or product. “The electricity a product uses when switched on” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In the waste hierarchy, which strategy generally gives the greatest environmental benefit?
items.correct: Reducing the amount of material and energy used in the first place
items.misconception: Recycling materials at end of life
items.why: Correct answer: Reducing the amount of material and energy used in the first place. “Recycling materials at end of life” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Recycling aluminium uses about 5% of the energy needed to produce it from ore. What percentage of the energy is saved?
items.why: Exact answer: 95 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
